title.en: VMs & Scale Sets: Sizing, Availability, Spot

description.en: Go beyond 'create a VM': pick the right size family, design for availability zones and update domains, autoscale with VM Scale Sets, and know when Spot instances are a bargain versus a trap.

story_variants.en:
## IaaS Is Not Legacy — It Is a Contract

Virtual machines remain the workhorse of enterprise Azure: lift-and-shift estates, databases with exotic licensing, GPU fleets, build agents. What separates a professional VM deployment from a hobby one is not the OS image — it is the *contract* you design around sizing, availability, and cost.

## Sizing: Families Are a Language

Azure VM size names encode meaning. Learn to read them and the catalog stops being noise:

| Family | Optimized for | Typical enterprise use |
|---|---|---|
| D-series (e.g., D4s_v5) | General purpose, balanced CPU:RAM (1:4) | App servers, web tiers |
| E-series | Memory optimized (1:8) | Caches, SAP app layer, in-memory analytics |
| F-series | Compute optimized (1:2) | Batch, game servers, CI agents |
| M-series | Massive memory (multi-TB) | SAP HANA, giant databases |
| L-series | Storage optimized, local NVMe | Scale-out data stores |
| N-series | GPU | ML training/inference, VDI rendering |
| B-series | Burstable, CPU-credit model | Dev boxes, low-duty services |

Suffixes matter: **s** = premium-storage capable (you almost always want it), **a** = AMD, **p** = ARM (Ampere), **d** = local temp disk. The 'v5/v6' generation bump often gives better price-performance than the size bump — re-benchmark on new generations before scaling up the old one.

> **Callout:** vCPU quota is granted per family per region per subscription. Landing a 100-vCPU GPU job fails at deploy time if nobody requested N-series quota — file quota increases as part of environment onboarding, not on launch day.

## Availability: Zones, Sets, and the Numbers That Matter

Azure's compute SLA is tiered by how you deploy:

- **Single VM with Premium SSD**: 99.9% SLA.
- **Availability set** (fault domains + update domains inside one datacenter): 99.95%. Fault domains isolate rack/power failures; update domains stagger planned host maintenance.
- **Availability zones** (physically separate datacenters in a region): **99.99%** for two or more VMs spread across zones. Zones are the modern default; availability sets are what you use in regions without zones.

Zone design has a cost dimension: cross-zone traffic and zone-redundant disks have their own pricing models, and not every size family exists in every zone. Check `az vm list-skus --zone` before promising an architecture.

## VM Scale Sets: Cattle at the IaaS Layer

A **VM Scale Set (VMSS)** manages a fleet of identical (or, in **Flexible orchestration** mode, mixed) VMs with autoscaling, automatic OS image upgrades, and zone spreading. Flexible mode is the modern default: up to 1,000 instances, mix Spot and standard, mix sizes, and still get fault-domain spreading.

Autoscale rules read platform metrics (CPU, queue depth, custom Application Insights metrics) and add/remove instances between your **min** and **max**. Two design rules keep autoscale from oscillating:

1. Use a **cooldown** so a scale-out has time to absorb load before the next evaluation.
2. Keep the scale-in threshold well below the scale-out threshold (e.g., out at >70% CPU, in at <30%) — a narrow band produces flapping.

> **War story:** An e-commerce team set scale-out at CPU>60 and scale-in at CPU<55 with a 1-minute window. Every marketing email produced a sawtooth of provision-then-destroy that never let instances warm their caches, and p95 latency got *worse* under autoscale than with a fixed fleet. Widening the band and lengthening cooldown to 10 minutes fixed what looked like an application bug.

## Spot: Rented at Eviction Risk

**Spot VMs** consume Azure's spare capacity at steep discounts — commonly quoted up to ~90% off pay-as-you-go, varying by size and region — with one catch: Azure can **evict** them with 30 seconds' notice when capacity is needed or your max price is exceeded. You choose the eviction policy: Deallocate (keep the disk, restart later) or Delete.

Spot is superb for: batch processing, CI agents, stateless render farms, dev/test fleets. Spot is a trap for: databases, quorum members, anything with a human waiting synchronously. The professional pattern is a VMSS with a **base capacity of standard VMs plus Spot overflow**, listening on the Scheduled Events endpoint (IMDS) to drain gracefully during the 30-second warning.

## Paying Less, Deliberately

Azure's compute pricing models, from most flexible to cheapest: **pay-as-you-go** → **savings plans** (hourly commitment, flexible across families) → **reservations** (1-/3-year, specific family/region, biggest discount) → **Spot** (deep discount, evictable). Add **Azure Hybrid Benefit** if you own Windows Server/SQL licenses with Software Assurance. The FinOps lesson later formalizes this; for now: steady-state = commit, spiky = autoscale, interruptible = Spot.

## Common Pitfalls

- Choosing sizes by vibes ("8 vCPUs sounds right") instead of load-testing one size up and one down.
- Forgetting the **temp disk** on 'd' sizes is ephemeral — data there dies on redeploy.
- Zone-pinning a single VM (you pay the complexity, get no SLA benefit) instead of spreading 2+.
- Autoscale on CPU when the real bottleneck is queue depth — scale on the metric your users feel.
- Running Spot without handling Scheduled Events, then calling evictions 'random crashes'.

## Mental Model

Treat a VM fleet like a portfolio: a committed base (reservations/savings plan) sized to the trough, an autoscaled middle sized to the daily wave, and a Spot layer for opportunistic work. Availability zones are your diversification across failure domains. When each layer has an explicit reason to exist, both your SLA math and your invoice become explainable — and 'explainable' is the real enterprise requirement.

description.en: A modern VMSS blends a standard base with Spot overflow, spread across zones, driven by autoscale rules.

left.label: Scale out at CPU>60, in at CPU<55 — constant flapping, cold caches

right.label: Out at >70, in at <30, 10-minute cooldown — stable fleet

description.en: A wide threshold band plus cooldown prevents autoscale oscillation, the most common VMSS misconfiguration.

animation_sequence.code:
az vmss create -g rg-web -n vmss-web \
  --image Ubuntu2204 --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --orchestration-mode Flexible \
  --zones 1 2 3 --instance-count 3 \
  --priority Spot --eviction-policy Deallocate

explanation.en: D4s_v5: general-purpose, premium-storage capable ('s'), current generation — the sensible web-tier default.
Flexible orchestration allows mixing Spot and standard instances and different sizes in one scale set.
Spreading across zones 1-3 lifts the compute SLA to the 99.99% tier.
Spot priority takes deep discounts but accepts 30-second eviction notice; Deallocate preserves disks for restart.

lines: # 30 seconds to drain connections and checkpoint work

description.en: The IMDS Scheduled Events endpoint is how a Spot instance learns it is about to be evicted — poll it and drain gracefully.
One full autoscale cycle, from surge to settle.

instruction.en: Simulate a VM Scale Set autoscaler. Start with instances = 2. Rules: if a CPU sample is greater than 70, scale OUT by 1 (action 'out', max 5 instances); if less than 30, scale IN by 1 (action 'in', min 2); otherwise action 'hold'. Implement step(instances, cpu) returning (action, new_instances). Process METRICS in order, printing for each sample exactly: 'cpu=<value> action=<action> instances=<count-after>'. After the loop print 'final=<count>'. Deterministic autoscale math like this is exactly what Azure Monitor autoscale evaluates every minute.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
METRICS = [45, 75, 82, 90, 65, 25, 20, 15]
MIN_INSTANCES, MAX_INSTANCES = 2, 5

def step(instances, cpu):
    # TODO: return (action, new_instances) applying the rules:
    # cpu > 70 -> 'out' (+1, capped at MAX_INSTANCES)
    # cpu < 30 -> 'in'  (-1, floored at MIN_INSTANCES)
    # else     -> 'hold'
    pass

instances = 2
# TODO: loop METRICS, call step, print 'cpu=.. action=.. instances=..'
# then print 'final=<instances>'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
cpu=45 action=hold instances=2
cpu=75 action=out instances=3
cpu=82 action=out instances=4
cpu=90 action=out instances=5
cpu=65 action=hold instances=5
cpu=25 action=in instances=4
cpu=20 action=in instances=3
cpu=15 action=in instances=2
final=2

en: Use min(instances + 1, MAX_INSTANCES) and max(instances - 1, MIN_INSTANCES) to enforce the fleet bounds.
The action is 'out' or 'in' based on the threshold even if the count is already at the cap — but with these metrics the cap is never exceeded, so simple math works.
Reassign instances with the value returned from step() before printing, so the printed count is the post-action fleet size.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert step(2, 45) == ('hold', 2)
assert step(2, 75) == ('out', 3)
assert step(5, 90) == ('out', 5)
assert step(2, 10) == ('in', 2)
assert step(4, 25) == ('in', 3)
inst = 2
for m in METRICS:
    _, inst = step(inst, m)
assert inst == 2

q.en: Which deployment earns the 99.99% compute SLA tier?

en: A single VM with Premium SSD
Two or more VMs spread across availability zones
Any VM inside a resource group with a lock
A single Spot VM with Deallocate policy

q.en: How much warning does Azure give before evicting a Spot VM?
In the size name Standard_E16s_v5, what does the E family indicate?
Why should scale-out and scale-in thresholds be far apart with a cooldown?

en: To prevent flapping — rapid provision/destroy cycles that hurt latency
Narrow bands are rejected by the API
Cooldowns are required for zone-spread scale sets only
